1	import { z } from "zod"
2	
3	// Note: if you edit the schema here, you must also edit the schema in the
4	// apps/v4/public/schema/registry-item.json file.
5	
6	export const registryConfigItemSchema = z.union([
7	  // Simple string format: "https://example.com/{name}.json"
8	  z.string().refine((s) => s.includes("{name}"), {
9	    message: "Registry URL must include {name} placeholder",
10	  }),
11	  // Advanced object format with auth options
12	  z.object({
13	    url: z.string().refine((s) => s.includes("{name}"), {
14	      message: "Registry URL must include {name} placeholder",
15	    }),
16	    params: z.record(z.string(), z.string()).optional(),
17	    headers: z.record(z.string(), z.string()).optional(),
18	  }),
19	])
20	
21	export const registryConfigSchema = z.record(
22	  z.string().refine((key) => key.startsWith("@"), {
23	    message: "Registry names must start with @ (e.g., @v0, @acme)",
24	  }),
25	  registryConfigItemSchema
26	)
27	
28	export const rawConfigSchema = z
29	  .object({
30	    $schema: z.string().optional(),
31	    style: z.string(),
32	    rsc: z.coerce.boolean().default(false),
33	    tsx: z.coerce.boolean().default(true),
34	    tailwind: z.object({
35	      config: z.string().optional(),
36	      css: z.string(),
37	      baseColor: z.string(),
38	      cssVariables: z.boolean().default(true),
39	      prefix: z.string().default("").optional(),
40	    }),
41	    iconLibrary: z.string().optional(),
42	    rtl: z.coerce.boolean().default(false).optional(),
43	    menuColor: z
44	      .enum([
45	        "default",
46	        "inverted",
47	        "default-translucent",
48	        "inverted-translucent",
49	      ])
50	      .default("default")
51	      .optional(),
52	    menuAccent: z.enum(["subtle", "bold"]).default("subtle").optional(),
53	    aliases: z.object({
54	      components: z.string(),
55	      utils: z.string(),
56	      ui: z.string().optional(),
57	      lib: z.string().optional(),
58	      hooks: z.string().optional(),
59	    }),
60	    registries: registryConfigSchema.optional(),
61	  })
62	  .strict()
63	
64	export const configSchema = rawConfigSchema.extend({
65	  resolvedPaths: z.object({
66	    cwd: z.string(),
67	    tailwindConfig: z.string(),
68	    tailwindCss: z.string(),
69	    utils: z.string(),
70	    components: z.string(),
71	    lib: z.string(),
72	    hooks: z.string(),
73	    ui: z.string(),
74	  }),
75	})
76	
77	// TODO: type the key.
78	// Okay for now since I don't want a breaking change.
79	export const workspaceConfigSchema = z.record(configSchema)
80	
81	export const registryItemTypeSchema = z.enum([
82	  "registry:lib",
83	  "registry:block",
84	  "registry:component",
85	  "registry:ui",
86	  "registry:hook",
87	  "registry:page",
88	  "registry:file",
89	  "registry:theme",
90	  "registry:style",
91	  "registry:item",
92	  "registry:base",
93	  "registry:font",
94	
95	  // Internal use only.
96	  "registry:example",
97	  "registry:internal",
98	])
99	
100	export const registryItemFileSchema = z.discriminatedUnion("type", [
101	  // Target is required for registry:file and registry:page
102	  z.object({
103	    path: z.string(),
104	    content: z.string().optional(),
105	    type: z.enum(["registry:file", "registry:page"]),
106	    target: z.string(),
107	  }),
108	  z.object({
109	    path: z.string(),
110	    content: z.string().optional(),
111	    type: registryItemTypeSchema.exclude(["registry:file", "registry:page"]),
112	    target: z.string().optional(),
113	  }),
114	])
115	
116	export const registryItemTailwindSchema = z.object({
117	  config: z
118	    .object({
119	      content: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
120	      theme: z.record(z.string(), z.any()).optional(),
121	      plugins: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
122	    })
123	    .optional(),
124	})
125	
126	export const registryItemCssVarsSchema = z.object({
127	  theme: z.record(z.string(), z.string()).optional(),
128	  light: z.record(z.string(), z.string()).optional(),
129	  dark: z.record(z.string(), z.string()).optional(),
130	})
131	
132	// Recursive type for CSS properties that supports empty objects at any level.
133	const cssValueSchema: z.ZodType<any> = z.lazy(() =>
134	  z.union([
135	    z.string(),
136	    z.array(z.union([z.string(), z.record(z.string(), z.string())])),
137	    z.record(z.string(), cssValueSchema),
138	  ])
139	)
140	
141	export const registryItemCssSchema = z.record(z.string(), cssValueSchema)
142	
143	export const registryItemEnvVarsSchema = z.record(z.string(), z.string())
144	
145	// Font metadata schema for registry:font items.
146	export const registryItemFontSchema = z.object({
147	  family: z.string(),
148	  provider: z.literal("google"),
149	  import: z.string(),
150	  variable: z.string(),
151	  weight: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
152	  subsets: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
153	  selector: z.string().optional(),
154	  dependency: z.string().optional(),
155	})
156	
157	// Common fields shared by all registry items.
158	export const registryItemCommonSchema = z.object({
159	  $schema: z.string().optional(),
160	  extends: z.string().optional(),
161	  name: z.string(),
162	  title: z.string().optional(),
163	  author: z.string().min(2).optional(),
164	  description: z.string().optional(),
165	  dependencies: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
166	  devDependencies: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
167	  registryDependencies: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
168	  files: z.array(registryItemFileSchema).optional(),
169	  tailwind: registryItemTailwindSchema.optional(),
170	  cssVars: registryItemCssVarsSchema.optional(),
171	  css: registryItemCssSchema.optional(),
172	  envVars: registryItemEnvVarsSchema.optional(),
173	  meta: z.record(z.string(), z.any()).optional(),
174	  docs: z.string().optional(),
175	  categories: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
176	})
177	
178	// registry:base has a config field, registry:font has a font field.
179	export const registryItemSchema = z.discriminatedUnion("type", [
180	  registryItemCommonSchema.extend({

Now I have a solid understanding of the codebase. Let me plan the Stepper component:

### Stepper Component Design

The Stepper component will be a pure React compound component (no Radix UI primitive needed for this) that uses React Context to share state between sub-components.

**Sub-components:**
1. `Stepper` - Root container, manages state (current step, orientation)
2. `StepperItem` - Individual step item container
3. `StepperTrigger` - Clickable trigger for each step
4. `StepperTitle` - Title of a step


5. `StepperDescription` - Description of a step
6. `StepperSeparator` - Visual separator between steps
7. `StepperContent` - Content panel for each step's body

**Features:**
- `orientation` prop: `"horizontal"` | `"vertical"` (default: `"horizontal"`)
- Controlled and uncontrolled modes (`value`/`defaultValue`/`onValueChange`)
- Step states: `active`, `completed`, `inactive`
- React Context for state sharing
- `data-slot` attributes for all components
- ARIA attributes for accessibility
- `data-state` attribute for styling (active/completed/inactive)
- Support for `linear` mode (can only go forward through steps)

The context will expose the active step, orientation, and a setter function to update which step is active. I'm sketching out the API structure with a root `Stepper` component wrapping individual `StepperItem` elements, each containing a trigger, separator, and content area. Looking at how tabs and similar components handle this pattern, I should consider whether the structure needs adjustment. Building the stepper component structure...

I'm realizing the user wants content panels for each step, so I need to decide whether to nest the content inside each StepperItem or keep it separate like a Tabs pattern. Looking at common UI library approaches, I could structure it either way—content nested within each item, or a separate content area that switches based on the active step value.

I think the Tabs-like pattern makes more sense here: the step triggers stay in a header area, and the StepperContent panels live outside, matched by their value prop. This gives more flexibility for layout and styling. I'm reconsidering the API design - should the step number be explicit as a prop, or should it be inferred from the order of items, and how should I handle the relationship between the trigger and content sections? I'm settling on a design where `StepperItem` provides its own context with the step value, state (active/completed/inactive), and position, while `StepperTrigger`, `StepperTitle`, `StepperDescription`, `StepperSeparator`, and `StepperContent` all read from these contexts to determine their behavior and appearance. The tricky part is figuring out how the root `Stepper` component tracks step order—I could require an explicit `steps` array prop, auto-compute it from DOM order, or just use string comparison, but I'm leaning toward having the user provide the ordered `steps` array for clarity and to determine which steps are completed based on their position relative to the current active step. I'm realizing the numeric step approach requires knowing the total upfront, which adds complexity. Let me simplify this by following how the Tabs component works instead—using a value-based system where each StepperItem has a unique identifier and the Stepper tracks the current value.

The cleanest solution is to have the user pass a steps array to the root Stepper component, so the parent knows the complete step sequence and can determine each step's state (active, completed, or inactive) based on its position in that array and the current active value. Looking at how Tabs works and what would feel natural for a wizard interface, I think the cleanest API would use string values like "step-1" and "step-2" to match shadcn's style, with a nested structure that includes triggers, titles, descriptions, and separators between each step.

Now I'm thinking through how to handle the completed state — I'll keep it simple by storing a steps array on the root Stepper component.

Actually, I should reconsider. Instead of pre-defining steps, I could use a registration pattern where each StepperItem registers itself when it mounts, and the order of registration determines the sequence.

But that's overcomplicating things. The cleanest approach is to have the user pass a steps array to Stepper that defines the ordered list, then track the active step via value/defaultValue/onValueChange. Each StepperItem references its value, and I compute whether it's active, completed, or inactive based on the steps array and current value. The indicator displays the step number for active and inactive states, and a checkmark for completed steps.

Now I'm setting up the component structure with a context to share the stepper state and configuration across child components. Building the Stepper component...

I need to reconsider how the layout should work depending on orientation. For a horizontal stepper, the step indicators should be arranged in a row with the content area below them. For a vertical stepper, the indicators would be in a column with content positioned to the side or below each step.

Looking at how shadcn's Tabs component handles this gives me a good pattern to follow—using `flex-col` for horizontal orientation and `flex-row` for vertical, which keeps the structure consistent and predictable.

For the separator behavior, I need to account for the orientation: horizontal steppers show separators as lines between items in the row, while vertical ones display them as lines running down between each step trigger. 

Now I'm thinking through the component structure itself. The root Stepper needs to manage the overall layout with flexbox based on orientation and handle the value state. The StepperList should arrange the triggers appropriately—as a row for horizontal and a column for vertical. Though looking back at the requirements, there's no explicit mention of needing a separate list component, so I should reconsider whether that's necessary or if I can structure this more simply.

Actually, I'm realizing the Stepper could serve double duty as both the container and the step list, with StepperContent panels sitting as siblings. But then the content panels need a way to know their position in the layout. Looking at how Tabs handles this pattern—with TabsList containing the triggers and TabsContent panels as separate children—that's probably the cleaner approach to follow here too.

Building the Stepper component structure... I'm working through the step registration approach, weighing the tradeoffs between using state callbacks versus refs to avoid unnecessary re-renders during the registration process.

The cleanest solution seems to be using numeric step values throughout the entire component API, so users just pass a number to each `StepperItem` rather than managing both a string value and a separate step prop. This eliminates the redundancy and keeps the API simple.

Actually, I'm reconsidering—what if someone prefers string values like they would with a Tabs component? I could support both by having the root `Stepper` accept a `steps` array that defines the ordering, so the comparison logic for determining active/completed/inactive states works regardless of whether values are strings or numbers.
